expectations.text: Creates a SINGLE Rust crate (not multiple) that exports all image effect functions
expectations.evidence: Cargo.toml defines a single crate named 'bentopdf-image-effects'. All functions are in one lib.rs file with #[wasm_bindgen] exports. One build.sh producing one .wasm binary. No separate crates.

expectations.text: Exports at minimum: apply_greyscale, apply_invert, apply_color_adjustments, apply_scanner_processing
expectations.evidence: lib.rs exports via #[wasm_bindgen]: apply_greyscale (line 13), apply_invert_colors (line 33), apply_color_adjustments (line 281), apply_scanner_pixels (line 191). Additionally exports granular functions: apply_brightness (line 49), apply_contrast (line 65), apply_sepia (line 83), apply_gamma (line 109), apply_temperature (line 128), apply_tint (line 148), apply_yellowish (line 165). All four required exports are present (apply_scanner_pixels serves as apply_scanner_processing).

expectations.text: One TypeScript loader and one WasmProvider entry for the unified module
expectations.evidence: One TypeScript loader exists: image-effects-wasm.ts provides loadImageEffectsWasm(), isImageEffectsWasmLoaded(), clearImageEffectsWasmCache(). However, there is NO WasmProvider integration. The loader resolves URLs independently via import.meta.env.VITE_WASM_IMAGE_EFFECTS_URL (line 58) and a fallback path (line 63), bypassing the existing WasmProvider system entirely. There is no WasmProvider entry, no update to the WasmPackage type union, and no integration with the existing WASM management infrastructure.

expectations.text: Rust code is organized into internal modules (e.g., mod greyscale, mod color) — not all in one file
expectations.evidence: All Rust code is in a single lib.rs file (613 lines / 491 lines of code). There are no 'mod' declarations, no separate .rs files, and no internal module organization. Helper functions (clamp_u8, clamp_f32, rgb_to_hsl, hsl_to_rgb, hue2rgb) are defined as private functions at the bottom of lib.rs (lines 395-490). Everything -- exports, helpers, color conversion, tests -- is in one monolithic file.

expectations.text: Addresses multi-hundred-page use case — discusses or implements batch processing, buffer reuse, or in-place mutation
expectations.evidence: All exported functions use in-place mutation via &mut [u8] parameters (e.g., apply_greyscale line 13, apply_color_adjustments line 281). The combined pipeline functions (apply_scanner_pixels and apply_color_adjustments) process all effects in a single pass over the buffer, reducing memory bandwidth. The integration-guide.md explicitly discusses this: 'apply_scanner_pixels and apply_color_adjustments run multiple effects in a single pass over the pixel buffer. This avoids multiple iterations and reduces memory bandwidth pressure -- the main bottleneck for these operations.' (line 91). The image-effects.ts includes a preloadImageEffectsWasm() function (line 68) for eager loading. Tests include a large buffer test (image-effects-wasm.test.ts line 101-129, testing 100x100 image).

overall_notes: The without_skill run creates a functional single-crate WASM module with good coverage of all required exports and additional granular functions. However, it has two notable gaps: (1) all Rust code is in one monolithic lib.rs file (491+ lines) with no internal module organization -- no mod declarations, no separate .rs files, violating the structure_check assertion; (2) the TypeScript loader bypasses the project's existing WasmProvider system, using its own URL resolution instead of integrating with WasmProvider.isConfigured() / WasmProvider.getUrl(). Strengths include: comprehensive JS fallback in image-effects.ts (full drop-in replacement), good test suite, and a detailed integration guide. 3 of 5 assertions pass.
